votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the logic to find the closest integer, including the specific tie-breaking rule (rounding away from zero) defined in the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly implements the logic to find the closest integer, including the specific tie-breaking rule (rounding away from zero) defined in the Coq specification. It calculates the floor and ceiling, compares distances, and uses absolute value comparison to resolve ties, which matches the formal requirements.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if the result is the closest integer to the input, and in case of a tie, choosing the one with the larger absolute value (rounding away from zero).

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It finds the closest integer to r, and when equidistant (dist_floor == dist_ceil), it picks the one with larger absolute value (away from zero). This matches the Coq spec which requires res to be closest to r (condition 1) and in case of ties, res must have absolute value >= any other equally-close integer (condition 2). The Python implementation computes the expected result directly and checks if res matches it, which is equivalent to verifying the Coq conditions for all concrete inputs.
